title: Bye Bye Nerdy

jokes.text: Itchy and Scratchy breakfast commercial with violence-themed cereal
jokes.setup: Cartoon characters making breakfast for kids
jokes.punchline: The cereal is literally violent weapons - pink daggers, green hatchets, yellow ice picks
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons dark humor - turning children's cereal commercial into weapon showcase. Strong character integration with Itchy & Scratchy's violent nature.

jokes.text: Pink daggers! Green hatchets! Yellow ice picks! And Stabby-Oh's are part of this nutritious breakfast.
jokes.setup: Cartoon characters making breakfast cereal that appears colorful and fun
jokes.punchline: The cereal pieces are literally weapons - daggers, hatchets, ice picks - marketed as nutritious
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark satire of cereal marketing - turning violence into breakfast food with cheerful advertising speak. Economy of language, escalating weapon names.

jokes.text: But thanks to Stabby-Oh's, most of me is still in bed.
jokes.setup: Marge appears normal while talking about the violent cereal
jokes.punchline: She's partially dismembered/injured from the cereal, treating it casually
jokes.explanation: Effective continuation of the cereal violence joke with Marge's deadpan delivery about being injured.

jokes.text: That ad campaign may have crossed a line. Ah, what can you do? Sex sells.
jokes.setup: Homer acknowledging the violent cereal advertising is problematic
jokes.punchline: He completely misattributes the appeal to sex rather than violence
jokes.explanation: Homer's characteristic obliviousness - wrong reason for the appeal but delivered with his typical confidence.

jokes.text: But I gotta spit. You can spit on the bus.
jokes.setup: Family rushing to catch the school bus while still eating
jokes.punchline: Marge's practical but gross solution to Bart's spitting problem
jokes.explanation: Marge's desperate parenting logic - functional but mildly disgusting solution.

jokes.text: Aw, darn. Looks like we'll have to stay home today.
jokes.setup: Missing the school bus
jokes.punchline: Bart's fake disappointment about missing school
jokes.explanation: Very predictable joke that any kid character might make. Low originality but fits Bart.

jokes.text: I'm not racing! It's me, Marge Simpson. No. You eat my dust! Hoo-hoo!
jokes.setup: Marge trying to catch the school bus driver's attention
jokes.punchline: She immediately contradicts herself and gets competitive about racing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment - starts responsible, gets swept up in competition. Character-specific behavior.

jokes.text: Whoa! It's like Speed 2... only with a bus instead of a boat.
jokes.setup: Dangerous bus chase scene happening
jokes.punchline: References the inferior Speed sequel but makes it even more mundane
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference that adds absurdity - comparing action scene to already-mocked sequel.

jokes.text: I won. Don't take that away from me.
jokes.setup: Marge successfully caught the bus after a chase
jokes.punchline: Kids point out they're already at school, but Marge desperately wants her victory
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment - Marge's rare moment of selfish pride, perfectly delivered line.

jokes.text: Red hair? What's she trying to pull? Those shoes look Canadian. She'll never fit in.
jokes.setup: Kids evaluating the new student Francine
jokes.punchline: Absurd criteria for exclusion - red hair as suspicious, shoes being 'Canadian'
jokes.explanation: Good satire of arbitrary social exclusion, with the Canadian shoes being particularly absurd.

jokes.text: One Hour Dry Cleaner? Man, that's fast.
jokes.setup: Serious conversation about helping the new kid
jokes.punchline: Bart completely ignores the conversation to marvel at a mundane sign
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart ADD moment - completely derails emotional moment with random observation.

jokes.text: Your baby is dead.
jokes.setup: Doorbell rings, Homer and Marge answer expecting normal visitor
jokes.punchline: Salesman opens with shocking death announcement
jokes.explanation: Brilliant shock opening - maximum impact with minimal words. Classic Simpsons dark humor structure.

jokes.text: Sorry about that. But the truth is... your baby, Maggie Simpson, is dead!
jokes.setup: Salesman apologizes for the scare tactic
jokes.punchline: Immediately repeats the same shock tactic
jokes.explanation: Good callback escalation - doubles down on the shock tactic for additional payoff.

jokes.text: That's a pretty big caboose for a baby. Homer, don't be-Wow, that is huge.
jokes.setup: Baby-proofer demonstrating by crawling around like a baby
jokes.punchline: Marge starts to scold Homer for commenting on the man's size, then agrees
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy - Marge's politeness cracking under obvious truth.

jokes.text: I always pictured the kids dying in the living room.
jokes.setup: Learning about kitchen dangers to babies
jokes.punchline: Homer casually mentions he's already imagined his children's deaths
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark Homer logic - treats child death as room planning problem with characteristic casualness.

jokes.text: That's the price of the clipboard. Here's your estimate.
jokes.setup: Homer and Marge relieved the price is only three dollars
jokes.punchline: That was just for the clipboard, real estimate is presumably much higher
jokes.explanation: Classic sales scam structure - builds false relief before the real shock.

jokes.text: Yeah! She thinks she's Babe: Pig in the City.
jokes.setup: Kids complaining about new girl hogging the teeter-totter
jokes.punchline: Specific, random movie reference that doesn't really fit the situation
jokes.explanation: Weak pop culture reference - random movie title without clear connection to the situation.

jokes.text: Not as long as I keep taking these.
jokes.setup: Lisa asking if Nelson remembers his first day at school
jokes.punchline: Implies he's taking memory-erasing drugs
jokes.explanation: Good dark character moment - Nelson's troubled past hinted at through drug reference.

jokes.text: Well, lots of people like jazz fusion. Okay. That's in the maybe file.
jokes.setup: Lisa suggesting ways to connect with the new girl
jokes.punchline: Jazz fusion is so unpopular even Lisa's mom puts it in 'maybe' file
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa character comedy - her intellectual interests being too niche even for bonding.

jokes.text: They're not dolls. They're aspiration figures.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting bonding over Malibu Stacy dolls
jokes.punchline: Lisa's pretentious correction about the nature of the toys
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa pretentiousness - intellectualizing children's toys with corporate speak.

jokes.text: She's not afraid of bunnies. She will be.
jokes.setup: Homer's plan to draw bunny faces on outlets to scare Maggie
jokes.punchline: Homer's ominous promise to make his baby fear bunnies
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark Homer moment - treating psychological manipulation of his baby as simple problem-solving.

jokes.text: I like jazz. Milhouse? She got you too? Yeah, but it's not so bad. I'm standing on Ralph.
jokes.setup: Lisa discovers Milhouse has also been bullied
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's literally standing on another bullied kid
jokes.explanation: Good visual escalation - reveals the absurd extent of the bullying through physical stacking.

jokes.text: We're a totem pole.
jokes.setup: Ralph being used as the base for Milhouse to stand on
jokes.punchline: Ralph's cheerful, matter-of-fact description of being bullied
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment - finds innocent joy in clearly abusive situation.

jokes.text: How are you supposed to dial? Reach into these holes. I use a carrot.
jokes.setup: Homer encased telephone in concrete for baby safety
jokes.punchline: His impractical carrot-based dialing solution
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Homer's safety obsession with characteristically absurd solution.

jokes.text: Baby could order poison. Oh, that's ridiculous. [Phone rings] Poison Delivery Service. A gift basket of poisons is on its way.
jokes.setup: Marge dismisses Homer's fear about baby ordering poison
jokes.punchline: Phone immediately proves Homer right with poison delivery call
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic timing - immediate proof of absurd claim with equally absurd service.

jokes.text: Oh, this is so funny. We were just talking about moving into protection.
jokes.setup: Bullies offered bodyguard work
jokes.punchline: They treat bullying/protection as legitimate career transition
jokes.explanation: Good satire of criminal career paths - treating bullying as professional development.

jokes.text: You sure this was done by hand? Yeah. She's a real purist.
jokes.setup: Examining quality of Indian burns on Lisa's arm
jokes.punchline: Treating torture technique as craft requiring authenticity
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy - professional assessment of torture methods like artisanal craft.

jokes.text: 'She'? Sorry. We don't do girls. They bite and kick and scratch. And sometimes we fall in love.
jokes.setup: Bullies refusing to fight girls for professional reasons
jokes.punchline: Escalates from practical concerns to romantic vulnerability
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from tough guy excuses to vulnerable admission about romance.

jokes.text: She? Sorry. We don't do girls. They bite and kick and scratch. And sometimes we fall in love.
jokes.setup: Learning the bully is female
jokes.punchline: Professional bullies have policy against female targets due to complications
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from practical to romantic concerns. Good character development for the bullies.

jokes.text: Did you know it predates agriculture?
jokes.setup: Lisa commenting on not understanding bullying history
jokes.punchline: Nelson provides unexpectedly scholarly historical context
jokes.explanation: Nice character subversion - Nelson showing unexpected academic knowledge about his own behavior.

jokes.text: That's like asking the square root of a million. No one will ever know.
jokes.setup: Lisa wondering why bullies target smart kids
jokes.punchline: Martin treats simple math as impossible mystery
jokes.explanation: Good Martin character moment - pseudo-intellectual response that's actually wrong.

jokes.text: No more Shiva-related pokings. Yeah. And, you know, down the line... you might want to switch to a nice, round Buddha.
jokes.setup: Homer baby-proofing Apu's religious statue
jokes.punchline: Casually suggests changing religions for safety reasons
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer ignorance - treating religious conversion as interior decorating decision.

jokes.text: But we are Hindu. So am I, but I don't get all huffy about it.
jokes.setup: Apu explaining they're Hindu in response to Buddha suggestion
jokes.punchline: Homer claims to be Hindu too but more casual about it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - claims religious affiliation while demonstrating complete ignorance.

jokes.text: 'Tis a mighty puddle of puke.
jokes.setup: Willie discovering vomit in hallway
jokes.punchline: Willie's poetic description of gross bodily fluid
jokes.explanation: Good Willie dialogue showing his poetic Scottish speaking style applied to gross situations.

jokes.text: Aye. Willie's a stinkin' liar.
jokes.setup: Willie denying existence of security cameras
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits to lying about it
jokes.explanation: Good Willie moment - his characteristic self-awareness about his own dishonesty.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. That roll of towels is nearing the end! Ach! It's on double red stripe!
jokes.setup: Lisa asking to see security tapes
jokes.punchline: Willie's dramatic overreaction to mundane supply issue
jokes.explanation: Good Willie character comedy - treating janitor supplies as life-or-death emergency.

jokes.text: Thank you, Drederick Tatum. That was truly a K.O. 'Knockout Oration.'
jokes.setup: Boxer finishing anti-violence school presentation
jokes.punchline: Skinner's forced pun combining knockout with oration
jokes.explanation: Weak pun but perfectly captures Skinner's awkward attempts at being cool.

jokes.text: You really don't want that. Trust me.
jokes.setup: Someone offering boxer a ride home
jokes.punchline: Boxer's ominous warning about the danger he poses
jokes.explanation: Good understated threat - self-aware danger warning delivered casually.

jokes.text: Nelson, what are you doing? I don't know. I can't help myself.
jokes.setup: Lisa's experiment exposing nerds to bully pheromones
jokes.punchline: Nelson compulsively starts bullying despite not wanting to
jokes.explanation: Good demonstration of Lisa's scientific discovery through character behavior.

jokes.text: Men. Boo! Canceled.
jokes.setup: Afternoon talk show beginning segment
jokes.punchline: Show immediately canceled after negative audience reaction
jokes.explanation: Good satire of reactive television programming - instant cancellation for political incorrectness.

jokes.text: You can dance You can dance Everybody look at your pants
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating being featured on TV
jokes.punchline: Butchers 'Safety Dance' lyrics with nonsense rhyme
jokes.explanation: Homer's characteristic song mangling - gets tune right but destroys lyrics.

jokes.text: Look at this baby. Not a scratch on him. And I got boat payments. The dream is over. Shut her down, boys.
jokes.setup: Baby safety movement hurting injury-related businesses
jokes.punchline: Business owner's despair over healthy babies ruining his livelihood
jokes.explanation: Dark economic satire - unintended consequences of safety creating perverse incentives.

jokes.text: Please, skin your knees. Put dice up your nose. Let cats sleep on your face!
jokes.setup: Homer realizing his safety crusade hurt baby-related businesses
jokes.punchline: Desperately begs babies to injure themselves
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy - Homer's complete 180 to economic desperation with specific injury suggestions.

jokes.text: That's quite an act to follow, Lisa. I know. And the crowd is so distinguished. The inventor of the walkie-talkie is out there. Where? Third row, near the aisle. Ooh. You're right. And that's not his wife.
jokes.setup: Science fair with distinguished audience
jokes.punchline: Escalates from professional recognition to personal scandal
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from professional to personal gossip about inventor's affair.

jokes.text: Pi is exactly three!
jokes.setup: Trying to get scientists' attention at noisy science fair
jokes.punchline: States mathematical heresy to shock them into silence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant scientist-specific attention-getter - mathematical incorrectness as shock tactic.

jokes.text: She's just a little girl. In the larval stage. Silly. Let's not listen.
jokes.setup: Lisa about to present scientific research
jokes.punchline: Scientists dismissing child with biological terminology
jokes.explanation: Good scientist character comedy - academic dismissal with entomological metaphor.

jokes.text: I call it 'Poindextrose.' Whoo-hoo! Simpsons rule! Sorry.
jokes.setup: Lisa naming her scientific discovery
jokes.punchline: Homer's inappropriate celebration followed by weak apology
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Homer's inappropriate pride disrupting serious presentation.

jokes.text: Are you mad, Frink? Put down that science pole!
jokes.setup: Professor Frink about to use scientific equipment
jokes.punchline: Treating scientific instrument as dangerous weapon
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment - 'science pole' as generic fearful term for equipment.

jokes.text: Actually, it's just ordinary salad dressing. So that's where that went.
jokes.setup: Bully repellent appears to be sophisticated scientific invention
jokes.punchline: Revealed as missing household condiment
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection reveal with Homer's practical domestic concern.

jokes.text: A gift certificate from JCPenney? Yes. You'll love their slacks.
jokes.setup: Lisa winning major science competition
jokes.punchline: Anticlimactic corporate gift certificate with mundane endorsement
jokes.explanation: Good anticlimax - major scientific achievement rewarded with corporate mediocrity.

jokes.text: No, Dad. Didn't you listen to anything I said? Just to get some attention.
jokes.setup: Lisa correcting Homer's misunderstanding of her research
jokes.punchline: Homer repeats the same wrong conclusion
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer not listening - perfect character consistency even after scientific explanation.

jokes.text: Don't worry. She'll punch herself out. Suck fist, Dr. Dork!
jokes.setup: Francine attacking Lisa without repellent
jokes.punchline: Homer's boxing metaphor while Francine delivers academic insult
jokes.explanation: Decent combination of Homer's casual violence observation with bully's academic insult.

jokes.text: Someone's gonna sleep well tonight. What a cutie. Look at her pound that nerd.
jokes.setup: Francine violently attacking Lisa
jokes.punchline: Observers treating violence as adorable entertainment
jokes.explanation: Dark commentary on society's casual attitude toward bullying violence.

tmdb_overview: When Lisa becomes the target of a female bully, Lisa discovers a scientific reason as to why bullies pick on nerds. Meanwhile, Homer goes into the baby-proofing business, which is threatening the businesses of pediatricians and greeting card salesmen.
